title: Handpicking — Separation of Substances
subject: Science
chapter: Curiosity Ch 9: Methods of Separation of Substances
concept: Handpicking: removing few, large, odd-coloured impurities (pebbles) from grains (wheat), and why it stops being convenient for large quantities.

additionalProps.hintText: That is a wheat grain — small, golden and oval with a groove down the middle. Pebbles are larger, dull grey and round.
additionalProps.promptAfterRound1: Quick and easy with just a few pebbles. But what if the heap were much bigger?

modes.label: Pick It
modes.description: Two-round guided flow: pick pebbles from a small pile, then a big pile, then a reflection on why handpicking suits only small quantities.

modes.label: Free Pile
modes.description: Free play: set grain and pebble counts with sliders, then time yourself clearing the thali.

learningObjectives: Identify handpicking as a separation method for small, visibly different impurities.
Distinguish wheat grains from pebbles by size, colour and shape.
Reason about why handpicking becomes inconvenient as quantity grows.
Connect the limitation to faster methods like winnowing and sieving.
